title: Good place to work with some downsides
body: Good place to work with some downsides
pros: Work-life balance has meaning at TR -- as a salaried employee, I rarely worked over 45 hrs/week. The company values families and flexibility, and its policies reflect that. Huge value is placed on innovation, and the products reflect that. I was proud to put the products we developed into the market.
cons: Constant upward growth is expected, and some senior leadership is pretty toxic. Often, a very strenuous goal was introduced with a minimal timeline for completion, and it wasn't unusual to be asked to deliver something completely untenable. Many, many talented people, including lots with law degrees and legal backgrounds, means the bar is very high for employees. Despite having a very talented workforce, salaries are often below market, and advancement can be challenging.

title: Prepare to be underpaid, underappreciated, and subjected to pointless rules
body: Prepare to be underpaid, underappreciated, and subjected to pointless rules
pros: The work is easy. You get a lot of vacation. You can work at home some days.
cons: Base salaries are terrible, and salary increases don't keep pace with inflation. The premium for exceptional performance is insultingly low (on the order of hundreds of dollars, not thousands), and is given as a one-time bonus, not an increase to your salary, so that salaries can be kept low. They require attorney editors to be in the office one day a week even though the job requires literally no interaction with other people. In general, they don't care about the quality of the work, and they don't care about keeping employees happy. It is an inflexible, authoritarian workplace whose primary goal is keeping salaries low and extracting obedience. Innovation is not welcome, and exceptional performance is not rewarded.

That is the difference now. The leaders came from a different segment of Thomson Reuters or from the outside and have no clue what selling our products to CPA firms is like. title: Thomson Reuters has gobbled up all of the local legal publishers.
body: Thomson Reuters has gobbled up all of the local legal publishers.
pros: The work was interesting. It was great to have access to the full suite of tools on Westlaw to do legal research.
cons: Thomson Reuters' m.o. is to acquire smaller local publishers and eventually shutter the location of the local publishers and move operations to Eagan, Minnesota. Now almost all jobs with TR are in Eagan. Who the heck wants to live there? TR also puts more and more responsibilities on managing editors without adjusting salaries. TR is anxious to create new online news portals but just as quick to axe them.

title: Fair pay; great benefits
pros: Fantastic benefits that really make a difference. An emphasis on work/life balance and respecting people's time away from work and encouraging reasonable work hours, etc. Some more progressive policies (like our parental leave, designed to more closely match our international coworkers) and LGBTQIA friendly. They equip you with the tools and resources you need to do your job productively. Generally speaking most people TR are friendly, easy to work with, and hard working but not INTENSE (all caps). Lots of opportunity for creativity or ingenuity within your role and leadership generally welcomes ideas and creative suggestions (at least in my org).
cons: Their current push via the CEO Steve Hasker for hybrid work is hypocritical while we also have programs and groups designed to encourage their overall "work to live not live to work" philosophy and their more progressive policies for working parents, neurodiverse folks, work from anywhere, etc. It also severely limits employees from growing their career with TR because hybrid roles require physical proximity to only a few places in the U.S. They also are a print company that became a technology company, but so much of their infrastructure is organized horizontally instead of vertically, and the product and tech teams aren't aligned with their front of house coworkers. Working on one part of one product creates a lot of unnecessary silos. They have an annual end of year "culling" of employees to hit shareholder expectations but justify it as being "nimble" or "responsive" - November is a rough month at TR.

What a horrible experience and how awful to feel like you don't matter. Apparently they have so much money, why not train a new team every day?

cons: -Stingy when it comes to actual cash pay and raises (as far as I can tell, this and the lack of transferable experience are what really drive the fairly-high turnover I witness in my role/team) -Heavy reliance on in-house software limits transferability of experience -Feels very bureaucratic at times: frequent upper management "restructures"

title: Wage theft
pros: Lots of paid time off
cons: Extremely low salaries. The pay froze in 2020 and raises never resumed more than a token level. Benefits were slashed starting in 2021. In 2023, when I started a new job, on the second month, the whole department was told we would not be paid until 2 months later (e.g. August rather than June). I never got paid for it. HR never resolved the issue and I left. I was never paid for the work I did in June. I had to get a second job just to cover food and cost of living with the recent inflation because pay was so low. I was a licensed attorney in a job that required licensure and training for many months and they still did not pay me a living wage. Positions for internal advancement are routinely taken down or not filled so applying for internal jobs is a very frustrating and disappointing process. There are also a lot fewer opportunities to advance beyond entry level than there used to be.

pros: The culture has been second to none since joining 8 years ago. The benefits they offer are amazing. The people I've had the pleasure of working with have become family. There's been so much flexibility at Thomson Reuters, which has allowed me to thrive in my career, while continuing to be a family man and dad to my two girls!

title: Great Culture Fueled by Great People
body: Great Culture Fueled by Great People
pros: Pay is competitive and benefits are outstanding, particularly the new unlimited PTO policy that's coupled with volunteer time, family leave, and more. With AI fueling new and revised products, it's an exciting time to be a part of Thomson Reuters.
cons: There are no cons at Thomson Reuters that don't exist in other corporate structures. Sometimes communication can be unclear or things occasionally fall through the cracks, but everyone from recruiters to HR to managers to executives does their best to live up to the high standards for trust that the company has set.

title: Difficult to move around
pros: I was legitimately the best person on my team. We are required to write 25 pages/week - I was writing 40 and maintaining our quality standards. I received an "exceeded" expectations 3 years in a row. They really give you the chance to get tons of writing experience. I worked there for 5 years because it's where I wanted my career to be. Tons of roles all over the world doing so many different things in so many different fields.
cons: Despite my best efforts, they gave me a 1.25% raise - AFTER I made my case for it. I was denied countless promotions or being moved to another team because I was heavy on writing experience and not much else. It's what made me leave.

title: If you value stability and clear direction then TR is NOT for you
body: If you value stability and clear direction then TR is NOT for you
pros: The people are great despite living in fear of the possibility of being let go at any moment
cons: There's an internal saying at TR... "every Thanksgiving you get a turkey and a re-org" which is sadly very true. Employees essentially live in continuous "fear" that they're going to be let go at the end of each year as part of TR's famous "RIF" (reduction in force) process or, worse, a more extensive re-org (This past year we had both). Another sad internal truism, "it's not a matter of IF but when you'll get let go from TR."

cons: Sometimes the goals are unrealistic for the sales force. As in, hardly anyone at all hits their annual quota, which suppresses compensation and drives off talent.

title: Terrible Management
pros: Benefits and work life balance are good
cons: The management team in the marketing department is terrible. If you like "yes men" you'll be happy. But if you think for yourself on anything you'll find this to be a struggle. Not only do the goal post constantly move, but the restrictions get tighter and tighter. The company changes yearly and causes major distribution for the first 2 to 3 months of the year. While everyoneis pissed off, you still have a quota to meet (and your pissed off too btw. Higher quota and no more money for doing more for less) And they are starting a new trend of send people's jobs overseas. They like cheap labor apparently, so don't be surprised if your job is outsourced as Jan 23.

cons: Most employees struggle to get noticeable raises, ever again, after the 12 month mark. Median length of career is only a year or two. Upper management like to look for layoffs every year around the holidays. The company is trying to outsouce every job it legally can, so morale is quite low. Understaffing is severe, leading to extremely stressful and intense customer interactions as a matter of routine. Production oversight is largely dysfunctonal, and departmental attrition is 3-4% per month, and has been for a few years.

title: Great Company. Great Benefits. Lack in Career Development.
body: Great Company. Great Benefits. Lack in Career Development.
pros: Worked at TR for over 10 years. Great culture overall. The people are wonderful and I felt I made a difference for our customers. The benefits are amazing and the company is rock solid. If you do a good job, they take care of you. I never worried about being let go and the health insurance really helped my family and I out a number of times as my kids were little.
cons: Tough to move around/up at the company around the time I left. Limited job openings since they brought in consultants and removed "layers" from 13 to 6. However, that didn't apply/stick for very long. I had my reports removed as did many other Managers and Directors got stuck with 10+ reports. But I still had to write reviews and manage the same people. I liked that aspect, but it was frustrating and felt like a demotion. There is a bit of a "club" at TR and if you are in, you are good. I saw this throughout my time there. Compensation is low compared to the market. HR became non-existent towards the end of my time there. Used to have ongoing conversations and helping manage people but I didn't even know who to talk to when I left. I felt the "focus on the customer" talking points started to fade in the last couple of years. Upper management would say it, but thats not what played out. It was more about money and contracts in my opinion.

title: Gold star
pros: I have been with the company for one year. In that time I have experienced respect for the individual, Camaraderie, and overall concern for the well-being of all employees at this company. They offer great benefits considering most companies' benefits are below the bare minimum. TR is always training and growing the knowledge of its workforce. They offer opportunities in other areas of the company. Over the course of the year, I have learned that some companies out there (TR specifically) really do work for the benefit of everyone, not just the upper management, I have seen that even the CEO carries himself with humility and grace. it's a pleasure to work here even during Peek seasons when stress is high and you feel tired from the back-to-back calls this is still an excellent place to work. I am grateful for the chance to be here.
cons: In the year I have been here my only complaint is scheduling and in that, I mean the last-minute changes to your schedule that you might miss if you do not check the schedule daily.

title: Excellent Company Who Invests In Employees For The Long Term
body: Excellent Company Who Invests In Employees For The Long Term
pros: Strong benefits package that goes well beyond health insurance and the usual. Strong training process that lets you hit the ground running. Reasonable expectations with regards to call volume, even in the short busy seasons of a few weeks in April and October. Company makes employees feel valued out of the gate, and has reasonable policies generally administered by actual humans with the ability to make decisions. Hours are perfectly reasonable, even in the busy season. "OT" means 4-8 hours a week at most, not the brutal meaning most companies attribute to it. The pay is above market, the position is permanently WFH. Company absolutely does not tolerate inappropriate behavior, but also encourages mutual understanding if there is a concern.
cons: Haven't found any to speak of so far. It's still early, but I'm looking forward to a career path. The default is 12 months in-role before applying for internal opportunities, but with manager/team lead approval, this can be shortened as/when appropriate.

pros: The best work life balance you can ask for. Managers understand that you have a life outside of your job, and respect that. At least with my experience. All managers are different. They have great perks for working at TR. An appreciate program where you earn points towards buying things off of their appreciate site.
cons: They are having issues keeping people at the company. Their salary is not as competitive. They did not give a raise this year to meet inflation. So technically I am making less that I was previous years. Even with the raise. They are not good at promoting. If the position is open you can apply, but just cause you have been in a role for so long is it enough to be promoted or create the next job level up to promote you.

pros: The flexibility can be a lot better than other places.
cons: The pat is terrible. The company shows patterns of favoritism. Itâs obvious how certain departments are looked down upon. For some reason, the company and CEO is obsessive about working in the office- even for jobs that are completely remote-type jobs. They are also obsessed with an âopen-floorâ concept of working, no matter how much it makes no sense for certain positions. Things like this that they refuse to listen to employees about is extremely frustrating. They will argue so much about it, and like most big companies, no matter how much they want you to think they care about your opinions, they mean nothing if they donât agree with them. For that reason, and the horrible pay, Iâm looking forward to searching for something else in this job market.

title: Not what it used to be...
body: Not what it used to be...
pros: The first 10+ years were amazing, then came the Change Program. Prior to Change Program, great work life balance. Great people doing the right thing for the customer. Lots of the opportunities to feel valued and make a difference.
cons: Since the Change Program the last 2 years it has been non-stop trying to get people to work more and harder. Working from home made it easy to just let people keep working long after they should have stopped. Large numbers of jobs moved to low cost locations regardless those locations being a good fit for the intended work. Great loss of institutional knowledge.

For example, if you get moved into new territory and are starting from scratch, you still need to hit your quota. Itâs like having 12-year ends, so thereâs always a mad scramble at the end of each month. Turnover is very high. Thomson Reuters has burned through a lot of great sales reps because of these things.

title: Great company, but too many changes
body: Great company, but too many changes
pros: Brand recognition Development opportunity/training resources Passionate people Great benefits package Good work/life balance.
cons: Compensation for current employees thousands behind other offers. Need more than low single digit cost of living bumps for retention. Having worked in the TR marketing department for 7 years, youâd expect some change. However, EVERY YEAR the company restructures due to new leadership vision, some external assessment or other unknown reasons â its breakneck. Marketing is expected to own the full funnel from awareness to closed deals, which is an unrealistic expectation for understaffed and funded teams. Leaders donât often listen to those actually working, or listen only to the high notes. The culture within smaller groups is overall good, but the top down is incredibly fuzzy to understand mission and goals beyond this yearâs sales target. The red tape created by âoperationsâ currently cripples marketing speed. With the resignations of 2021, a lot of new leaders trying to prove themselves.

cons: Market is becoming saturated. Large quota changes making quota difficult for even the most skilled reps. Upper management has made it clear that they are trying to automate the sales process for the "small law" market.\ Can be treated like a number.

title: Ship is sinking
pros: Brand Recognition, Years of industry experience
cons: Upper management, the products, and the sales talent is getting worse by the day.

title: Great Company and Job
pros: TR cares about its people and shows it in its approach to time off, benefits, and special events. It's not just all talk, which is very different from many other places. Communications from leaders is also really good - open, honest, practical, thoughtful, and positive. The job I have is fantastic - I am paid and respected for my experience, and my ideas are taking seriously. While it is a fairly flat organization so moving up would be a challenge, there are plenty of opportunities to work on special projects, rotate to other groups, and continue to learn, develop and grow, regardless of the state in your career. And the pay and benefits are fantastic, particularly given the low stress environment relative to other legal jobs. You can really get work-life balance at TR in the editor role, and I've really enjoyed it.
cons: For someone who is looking to have work-life balance and share knowledge and experience, this is a great job. But maybe not for someone who is still looking to climb the ladder in their career because the org structure is pretty flat. People are happy here so they don't leave, which means that it is hard to move up, at least in the part of TR's business that I am in, which is Practical Law.

title: Good people bad environment
pros: The people you get to work with make it an amazing place to be. Most of your co-workers are willing to lend a hand in helping you to succeed. Good product and control of market.
cons: No training, both sales and product training. You get about 2 weeks in total and have to figure it out as you go for most part. Works for some people, but definitely not the best way to go about it. Middle management for SDRs and other roles is not great. Some of the managers I worked with had a lot on their plate and therefore could not dedicate the appropriate amount of time to helping their employees succeed. Also during 1 on 1 meetings and such, the attention paid by managers was lacking. Its a very sink or swim place, and it does weed out the weaker links, but I think they could find a bit of a better balance.

title: Checked out managers
pros: The company seems to want to do the right thing by staff. But comes up short.
cons: This place is psychological pollution. My manager was on maternity leave (her third one in a row) and let me sit for six months with nothing to do. No onboarding. I had to bug the rest of the team for things to do. In the year I was there I saw constant changes, chaos, and a general cutthroat environment. Managers throw employees under the bus for their own career gain. â¦.at least mine did. She was the worst manager Iâve ever had. It is such a waste of talent and company money to not develop the team. There is a code among managers that no matter how incompetent they are, they protect each other. Why doesnât TR send out manager surveys fir the teams to provide feedback? Because they donât care about the employee experience whatsoever!

pros: Big company with a lot of options for other roles if you want to learn new skills or try something else. People are really âgood peopleâ, you donât find that very often. Willing to spend money on things or technology that will help it grow or make things easier/better. Very good community partner with local events, and invest quite a bit in people here.
cons: Wall between upper executive team and everyone else (which may be a good thing), Basically we hire consulting companies to run the place. WAY too many initiatives running all the time that distract us from doing our job. Also trying so hard to be inclusive we sometime forget the workplace part.

title: Fast-Changing Company - High Customer and Employee Focus
body: Fast-Changing Company - High Customer and Employee Focus
pros: After some recent major structural changes, the company is now more focused on being an "information" company - focusing on its strengths. This is truly a global company, and, depending on your specific work, it is not uncommon to work with counterparts in other countries. I also feel there is keen interest in trying to be on the bleeding edge of new technologies and ways of working.
cons: Along with the pros mentioned above comes a lot of change as the company seeks to reinvent itself. This can be exciting as new opportunities come from change, but it can also be a bit scary if you are not able or willing to adapt to the changes.

title: Leadership canât quite get it together
body: Leadership canât quite get it together
pros: Nice people, flexible schedules, a line of business you can feel proud to work in.
cons: We change direction every year. Initiatives that were a âtop priorityâ are nowhere to be seen 18-24 months later (agile). Focusing on external senior leadership hires that donât really care about the people- moving huge numbers of roles to India and Mexico. The company is moving through a Change Program on the backs of overworked employees across all parts of the company. If the company were willing to offer full-time remote or increase pay to align with market, perhaps we could find it worthwhile to stick it out. But that does not seem to be the case.

pros: You get paid a pay check you can technically stay alive off of. They have a reasonable cafeteria in their Ann Arbor office.
cons: Thomson Reuters has hands down the worst culture out of any organization that I've ever worked at. Leadership hires strictly out of favoritism without considering overall validity of the candidate they've hired. They also pay far below what their competitors pay for the same position.

title: Major Problems there! Longterm employees are heading for the hills!
body: Major Problems there! Longterm employees are heading for the hills!
pros: Big, global org - so opportunities to work in different areas of the company. Good initial Covid response.
cons: --Layoffs happen every single year and is now the go-to tool in Q4 to hit numbers. Company leverages a culture of fear of job loss against employees. --Old tools that don't work and aren't enterprise-wide. --Internal leadership survey results are ignored or hidden. --Subject matter experts are ignored. --Constant issues with internal tech. --Limited advancement opportunities. To move up or get raises it's common practices for people to leave the company and then come back at a higher salary for the same role. --Toxic culture - lack of diverse and female leaders. --Constant reorgs. CONSTANT. --new 'flat' hierarchy means no promotions.

title: Not the same TR from a decade+ ago
body: Not the same TR from a decade+ ago
pros: In my years working at TR, I've always been in awe of my amazing colleagues; they operate with integrity and professionalism, have great senses of humor, and are very inclusive. 2 out of my 3 immediate managers during my time at TR have all been great; ensuring that your family and your health come first. TR responded very well during the pandemic. Still, to date, no one is required to return to the office, and although people were encouraged to start returning in August/September 2021, now that the Covid numbers (due to the variant) are increasing, that is all on hold.
